title: Oat lace biscuits
subtitle: Thin, lacey biscuits that spread into golden rounds
excerpt: Twenty thin, crisp oat biscuits that spread flat in the oven into irregular, lacey rounds. They look fancy but take 10 minutes to prepare. Eat them as they are or wrap around a rolling pin while still warm to make curved tuile shapes.
sourceNotes: Lace biscuits made from oats and butter toffee are documented in Scandinavian and British baking from at least the nineteenth century. The form appears in pre-1928 Norwegian and Swedish household cookery texts (the Norwegian havrekjeks and Swedish havreflarn). A British version appears in Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136). Cross-referenced with early-twentieth-century Scandinavian domestic baking manuals available through Nordic library archives.

recipe.batchNotes: Bake one tray at a time; two trays in the oven at once bake unevenly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep in an airtight tin, layered with paper. Stay crisp for 5 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven. Conventional 185°C. Watch from 6 minutes; they colour quickly. Take them out when golden at the edges but still slightly pale in the centre.

recipeTools.notes: For shaping warm biscuits into tuiles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Lace biscuits spread dramatically in the oven because they contain a high proportion of butter and sugar relative to flour. As the butter melts and the sugar liquefies, the mixture flows outward before the small amount of flour can set it in place. Leaving too much space between the mounds is important: each biscuit can double or triple in diameter. If the biscuits fuse together on the tray, they were too close. The lacey holes appear because the steam and sugar bubbling does not fill evenly across the surface.

content.text: Oat lace biscuits are a simple melt-and-mix that bakes into something disproportionately elegant: thin, almost transparent rounds with irregular holes, crisped to a toffee-like snap. The
is the recipe working correctly, not a mistake. Twenty biscuits. Space the mounds widely on the tray.

Heat the oven to 175°C fan (185°C conventional). Line two baking trays with baking paper. Melt the butter, sugar, and golden syrup together in a medium saucepan over low heat until the sugar dissolves and the mixture is smooth. Remove from the heat and stir in the oats, flour, and salt.

Drop level teaspoons of mixture onto the lined trays, spaced at least 8 cm apart; they will spread to at least double their original size. Bake one tray at a time for 7 to 9 minutes, until golden at the edges and set in the middle. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes before lifting; they will be too fragile when hot. Transfer carefully to a wire rack and leave until completely cold and crisp.
